title: Emerging 2D Materials for Beyond von Neumann Computing: A Perspective
abstract: The end of conventional Dennard scaling and the widening gap between memory bandwidth and arithmetic throughput have made the von Neumann partition a structural bottleneck rather than a transient one. Two-dimensional (2D) materials, with atomically thin geometries, electrically tunable carrier densities, and large optical responses, offer a unified platform on which to build devices that compute where they store, process events rather than clock cycles, and shift workload into the optical domain. This perspective surveys progress along three converging thrusts, graphene and graphene nanoribbon transistors as scalable channel materials, oxide and 2D-integrated memristors for in-memory analog compute, and silicon-compatible 2D photonic and thermal-emitter structures for optical computing primitives. Our central argument is that the 2D-materials community has spent a decade producing record devices, and the next decade will be decided by who first integrates three of them on a single semiconductor wafer.
summary: This perspective explores how 2D materials can overcome the von Neumann bottleneck by enabling new computing paradigms like in-memory computing and optical processing. It highlights the transition from individual device records to the critical challenge of large-scale integration on a single wafer.
main_problem: The von Neumann architecture faces a structural bottleneck due to the widening gap between memory bandwidth and arithmetic throughput, alongside the end of Dennard scaling.
main_result: 2D materials provide a unified platform for non-von Neumann computing by enabling in-memory analog computing, event-based processing, and optical computing primitives.
method: A perspective survey of progress in three technological thrusts: graphene-based transistors, 2D-integrated memristors, and silicon-compatible 2D photonics.
model_or_system: The paper focuses on 2D material systems including graphene, graphene nanoribbons, oxide/2D-integrated memristors, and 2D photonic/thermal-emitter structures.
main_assumptions: The central argument assumes that the future of the field depends on the successful integration of these diverse 2D components on a single semiconductor wafer.
paper_structure: The paper is structured as a perspective survey covering three converging thrusts: scalable channel materials (graphene), in-memory analog compute (memristors), and optical computing primitives (photonics/thermal emitters).
